corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We present a new method that views object detection as a direct set prediction problem.

top_candidates.sentence: We show that DETR achieves competitive results compared to Faster R-CNN in quantitative evaluation on COCO.
The model has 41.3M parameters, achieves 40.6 and 42.0 AP on short and long schedules respectively, and runs at 28 FPS, similarly to Faster R-CNN-FPN with the same backbone.
We show that DETR outperforms published results on COCO-val 2017, as well as our strong PanopticFPN baseline (trained with same data-augmentation as DETR, for fair comparison).
Current detectors required several years of improvements to cope with similar issues, and we expect future work to successfully address them for DETR.

Two ingredients are essential for direct set predictions in detection: (1) a set prediction loss that forces unique matching between predicted and ground truth boxes; (2) an architecture that predicts (in a single pass) a set of objects and models their relation.
The overall DETR architecture is surprisingly simple and depicted in Figure .
Unlike many modern detectors, DETR can be implemented in any deep learning framework that provides a common CNN backbone and a transformer architecture implementation with just a few hundred lines.
Unlike many modern detectors, DETR can be implemented in any deep learning framework that provides a common CNN backbone and a transformer architecture implementation with just a few hundred lines.
Each encoder layer has a standard architecture and consists of a multi-head self-attention module and a feed forward network (FFN).
Since the transformer architecture is permutation-invariant, we supplement it with fixed positional encodings that are added to the input of each attention layer.
We defer to the supplementary material the detailed definition of the architecture, which follows the one described in .
The decoder follows the standard architecture of the transformer, transforming N embeddings of size d using multi-headed self-and encoder-decoder attention mechanisms.

We note that almost all slots have a mode of predicting large image-wide boxes that are common in COCO dataset. simple ablations of different losses (using the same weighting every time), but other means of combining them may achieve different results.
We also evaluated our method on the test set of the COCO dataset, and obtained 46 PQ.

But since few to no COCO images contain only a lot of objects of the same class, this type of experiment represents our best effort to understand whether query objects overfit the label and position distribution of the dataset.
We describe our architecture in detail in Figure .
We present a new method that views object detection as a direct set prediction problem.

The main ingredients of the new framework, called DEtection TRansformer or DETR, are a set-based global loss that forces unique predictions via bipartite matching, and a transformer encoder-decoder architecture.

The main ingredients of the new framework, called DEtection TRansformer or DETR, are a set-based global loss that forces unique predictions via bipartite matching, and a transformer encoder-decoder architecture.
DETR demonstrates accuracy and run-time performance on par with the well-established and highly-optimized Faster R-CNN baseline on the challenging COCO object detection dataset.
DETR demonstrates accuracy and run-time performance on par with the well-established and highly-optimized Faster R-CNN baseline on the challenging COCO object detection dataset.

Their performances are significantly influenced by postprocessing steps to collapse near-duplicate predictions, by the design of the anchor sets and by the heuristics that assign target boxes to anchors .
To simplify these pipelines, we propose a direct set prediction approach to bypass the surrogate tasks.
We adopt an encoder-decoder architecture based on transformers , a popular architecture for sequence prediction.
Unlike most existing detection methods, DETR doesn't require any customized layers, and thus can be reproduced easily in any framework that contains standard CNN and transformer classes. .
